title: 01_ai_usage_guidelines
text: AI Usage Guidelines (Internal Knowledge Base Excerpt) Version: 1.3 | Last updated: 2025-09-18 Purpose These guidelines describe how teams may use AI tools for drafting, summarization, coding assistance, and analytics, while maintaining security, privacy, and quality standards. Key principles 1) Data classification - Public: may be used freely. - Internal: may be used only with approved AI services and access controls. - Confidential/Restricted: must not be sent to external services unless explicitly approved. 2) Prompt hygiene - Do not include secrets, API keys, passwords, or private customer identifiers in prompts. - Use placeholders (e.g., <API_KEY>, <CUSTOMER_ID>) and secure configuration stores. 3) Human review - AI outputs must be reviewed for accuracy, bias, and completeness before use in production materials. - Claims must be validated against source documents when the content is used for compliance, legal, or finance topics. 4) Logging and traceability - Where feasible, store the user question, retrieval sources, and final answer for auditability. - Maintain citation-style references (document id + section/chunk id) for grounded answers. Examples of acceptable use - Drafting email templates and internal communications - Summarizing internal documentation that is already approved for internal sharing - Generating unit-test scaffolding and refactoring suggestions (with review) Examples of prohibited use - Submitting private customer data without approval - Using AI output as a final decision for hiring, compensation, or disciplinary action

title: 02_rag_pipeline_runbook
text: RAG Pipeline Runbook (Simplified) Version: 2.0 | Last updated: 2025-10-02 Objective Enable users to query internal documentation and receive grounded answers with citations. High-level flow 1) Ingest documents - Sources: knowledge base articles, SOPs, runbooks, FAQs, product notes - Extract text and normalize whitespace - Attach metadata: doc_id, title, category, owner, updated_at 2) Chunking - Chunk by tokens (not characters) to respect model context limits - Recommended starting point: 400 tokens with 50-token overlap - Remove chunks with < 40 tokens unless they are critical headings 3) Embeddings + indexing - Generate embeddings for each chunk (same model for query and documents) - Store in a vector index (FAISS for local dev; managed vector store for production) - Persist metadata alongside embeddings: chunk_id, source, page/section, token_count 4) Retrieval - Embed the user query - Retrieve top-k by cosine similarity - Apply filters (e.g., product=AzureVDI, category=Networking) and access control rules - Optionally diversify results using MMR to avoid duplicates 5) Answer generation (grounded) - Provide the top passages to the chat model as "sources" - Instruct the model to answer only using sources, and cite references like [S1], [S2] - If sources do not contain the answer, the model should say it does not know Operational checks - Validate chunk quality (duplicates, too-short chunks, boilerplate) - Monitor retrieval accuracy using a small benchmark of questions - Track latency and cost drivers: token counts and embedding batch sizes

title: 03_vector_store_best_practices
text: Vector Store Best Practices (Notes) Last updated: 2025-08-11 Why a vector store A vector store enables fast similarity search over embeddings. This is the core building block behind semantic search and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Indexing recommendations - Normalize vectors if you want cosine similarity (cosine = dot product on normalized vectors). - Keep a stable mapping between the vector row and its metadata (doc_id, chunk_id, title, tokens). - Store enough metadata to support filtering: product, department, version, updated_at, ACL tags. Chunking guidance - Start with 200–800 token chunks and 10–20% overlap. - Use headings and structure when available (section-aware chunking) rather than purely fixed windows. - Avoid very small chunks unless you have a reranker or a strong metadata strategy. Retrieval tips - Retrieve more than you show (e.g., fetch_k=20) and then apply reranking/diversification to select final_k=5. - Use MMR to reduce redundancy when many chunks are similar. - Consider a simple keyword overlap score as a tie-breaker if semantic scores are close. Quality and safety - Do not embed secrets. - For regulated content, keep an audit trail: query, retrieved chunk ids, and answer with citations. - Re-embed only when documents change; otherwise reuse persisted indexes.
